coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of NH2-MIL-53(Al)@BiOBr@CQDs ternary heterostructure photocatalyst for degradation of ciprofloxacin in water
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Xinyue
Li, Yong
Yue, Deyun
Li, Rui
Song, Haojie
coredata.dc:description: The construction of ternary heterojunction photocatalysts can achieve multi-gradient transfer of charges and accurate control the position of photogenerated electrons which has received increasing attention. In this paper, NH2-MIL-53(Al)@BiOBr@CQDs-10 % (ABCQDs-10 %) was prepared via a facile hydrothermal and chemical synthesis processes. Three-dimensional serration of NH2-MIL-53(Al) as a platform for irregular BiOBr nanosheets, CQDs promote interfacial contact and act as electron transport channels between BiOBr and NH2-MIL-53(Al) and constitute Z-type heterojunctions with high redox properties. ABCQDs-10 % shows the best photocatalytic removal efficiency (94.24 %) of CIP, the reaction rate constants (0.045 min−1) were 1.2 and 64 times higher than BiOBr and NH2-MIL-53(Al), respectively. The excellent degradation performance is attributed to the construction of Z-type heterojunction of NH2-MIL-53(Al)@BiOBr@CQDs which improve electron-hole separation and transfer efficiency. The main active species was •O2 -. The photocatalytic activity of ABCQDs-10 % had not obvious loss after cycle experiments and the crystal structure had no significant changes, means high stable photocatalytic activity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Degradation of antibiotic
Z-type heterojunctions
Ternary system
Carbon quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Crystal structures, morphologies, and chemical compositions
The photocatalytic performance and photocatalytic mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of the preparation of NH2-MIL-53(Al)@BiOBr@CQDs.SEM images of BiOBr (b); NH2-MIL-53(Al) (c); ABCQDs-10 %(d); TEM images of the ABCQDs-10 % (e); HRTEM image of ABCQDs-10 % (f); EDS mapping of NH2-MIL-53(Al)@BiOBr@CQDs (g).
The XRD patterns (a–b) and FT-IR spectra (c–d); XPS spectra the high-resolution spectra (e–g): Br 3d, Bi 4f and N 1s; Nitrogen adsorption-adsorption isotherm and pore size distribution plots (h).
UV–vis DRS spectra (a–b); The (αhυ)1/2 versus, (αhυ)2 versus Eg curves (c); Mott-Schottky plots (d); Photoluminescence spectra (e) and Transient photocurrent densities (f).

Photocatalytic activity (a–b); first-order kinetic plots (c–d) and rate constants (e–f) of NH2-MIL-53(Al), BiOBr, and ABCQDs-x; Recycle test (g); Active species elimination experiments of ABCQDs-10 % (h); ESR spectra for DMPO-•O2 - (i); The mechanism of ABCQDs-10 % for CIP removal (j–k).

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of NH2-MIL-53(Al)@BiOBr@CQDs ternary heterostructure photocatalyst for degradation of ciprofloxacin in water

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science & Engineering, Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Green Preparation and Functionalization for Inorganic Materials, Shaanxi University of Science & Technology, Xi’ An, 710021, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Green Preparation and Functionalization for Inorganic Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science & Engineering, Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Green Preparation and Functionalization for Inorganic Materials, Shaanxi University of Science & Technology, Xi’ an, 710021, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The construction of ternary heterojunction photocatalysts can achieve multi-gradient transfer of charges and accurate control the position of photogenerated electrons which has received increasing attention. In this paper, NH2-MIL-53(Al)@BiOBr@CQDs-10 % (ABCQDs-10 %) was prepared via a facile hydrothermal and chemical synthesis processes. Three-dimensional serration of NH2-MIL-53(Al) as a platform for irregular BiOBr nanosheets, CQDs promote interfacial contact and act as electron transport channels between BiOBr and NH2-MIL-53(Al) and constitute Z-type heterojunctions with high redox properties. ABCQDs-10 % shows the best photocatalytic removal efficiency (94.24 %) of CIP, the reaction rate constants (0.045 min−1) were 1.2 and 64 times higher than BiOBr and NH2-MIL-53(Al), respectively. The excellent degradation performance is attributed to the construction of Z-type heterojunction of NH2-MIL-53(Al)@BiOBr@CQDs which improve electron-hole separation and transfer efficiency. The main active species was •O2 -. The photocatalytic activity of ABCQDs-10 % had not obvious loss after cycle experiments and the crystal structure had no significant changes, means high stable photocatalytic activity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, antibiotics are one of the most widely used in treatment of bacterial infectious diseases. Ciprofloxacin (CIP) as a regular antibiotic which has advantages in wide antimicrobial spectrum, low price and excellent antibacterial activity [1]. However, a variety of diseases caused by accumulation of antibiotics in the food chain, it is important that the occurrence of drug resistance have a serious risk to human health [2]. Currently, a variety of technologies to remove antibiotic pollutants from water are explored by scientists. Compared with the traditional water treatment, photocatalytic is16viewed as one of the most expected technologies, due to its properties of high-efficiency, green and environmentally friendly [3]. However, constructing high-activity photocatalysts is a great challenge in photocatalytic degradation of antibiotic [4].

Metal-organic frameworks (MOF) have the characteristic in large specific surface area, abundant coordinated unsaturated metal sites, and excellent adsorption performance [5]. However, low charge separation and high electron-hole pair complexation efficiency, which seriously affects photocatalytic performance of MOFs. Research of many scholars have shown that MIL-53(Al) has photocatalytic activity and stability, what's more, amino functionalized MIL-53(Al) raise the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron holes and broaden the light absorption range [6]. Bismuth oxybromide (BiOBr) has received considerable attention in photocatalysis due to its remarkable merits, including suitable bandgap, environmentally friendly, good photocatalytic activity as well as chemical stability [7]. Despite these advantages, the photocatalytic efficiency is still comparatively low due its high recombination frequency of carriers [8,9].

The combination of NH2-MIL-53(Al) and BiOBr can promote the separation of charge carriers, but easily form type II heterojunctions with low redox activity, reducing photocatalytic activity [10]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are carbon nanomaterials with the structure of zero-dimensional has excellent light response and electrical conductivity which can be acted as active sites of photocatalytic reactions, and energy barrier between semiconductors to prevent electronic backflow. Xie et al. [11] though hydrothermal and photoinduced methods fabricated novel heterojunction Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs. CQDs enhanced the light absorption ability, the Z-scheme heterojunctions structure promote the separation and transfer of photo-induced electron hole pairs. The construction of ternary heterojunction photocatalysts by CQDs can achieve multi-gradient transfer of charges and accurate control the position of photogenerated electrons [12–15].

Herein, Z-scheme NH2-MIL-53(Al)@BiOBr@CQDs (ABCQDs-10 %) was synthesized via facile hydrothermal and chemical synthesis processes for efficient degradation of Ciprofloxacin (CIP) pollutants. Carbon quantum dots act as electron acceptors, helping to transfer photogenerated electrons and holes between NH2-MIL-53(Al) and BiOBr, thus improving charge separation efficiency. Their composition, structure, morphology and light absorption properties by test and analysis. Subsequently, analyzed the degradation of CIP mechanism of the composite on CIP. In addition, the generation of active species was explored by a quenching experiment and deduced photocatalytic mechanisms of composite. This study provides new insight and supplies some guidance in photocatalytic degradation of CIP.

Aluminum nitrate nonahydrate (Al(NO3)3·9H2O), acetic acid, 2-aminotheraphthalic acid (NH2-BDC), N, N dimethylformamide (DMF), Urea, Citric Acid (C6H8O7), Bi(NO3)3⋅5H2O and KBr were obtained from Aladdin Co.

The canonical NH2-MIL-53(Al) was prepared by the solvothermal method. NH2-BDC (0.48 g) and Al (NO3)3·9H2O (1 g) added in 15 mL ultrapure water, respectively and ultrasound for 20 min. Then, Al(NO3)3·9H2O solution added in NH2-BDC solution and stirring for 30 min. The above mixture was transferred to a volume of 100 mL of Teflon liner after magnetic stirring, and reaction 150 °C for 4 h. The synthesized sample was washed with DMF and ethanol three times each, respectively. Finally, NH2-MIL-53(Al) power was obtained by vacuum drying at 70 °C.

The CQDs was prepared by the solvothermal method. Urea (1 g) and C6H8O7 (3 g) added in 10 mL ultrapure water and ultrasound and stirring for uniform dispersion. The above mixture was transferred to a volume of 100 mL of Teflon liner after magnetic stirring, and reaction 180 °C for 5 h. The resulting supernate was obtained by centrifugation at 10000 r/min for 30 min. Finally, CQDs solution stored in refrigerator of 5 °C.

For the synthesis of NH2-MIL-53(Al)@BiOBr@CQDs (designated as ABCQDs-x), KBr (5 mmol) and NH2-MIL-53(Al) added in 100 mL ultrapure water vigorous stirring was performed for 1 h, marked as solution A. Meanwhile, Bi (NO3)3⋅5H2O (5 mmol) and acetic acid (9 mL) added in 100 mL ultrapure water vigorous stirring was performed for 1 h, marked as solution B. The solution B was dissolved in solution A and CQDs solution (40 μL) under magnetic stirring for another 4 h, then stewing an hour. The synthesized composite materials were washed by ultrapure water and ethanol three times each, respectively. Lastly, NH2-MIL-53(Al)@BiOBr@CQDs power was obtained by drying at 60 °C. The synthesized composite materials were marked as ABCQDs-x, where the mass ratios of BiOBr and NH2-MIL-53(Al) were represented as x. BCQDs was synthesized in the same way as above without NH2-MIL-53(Al), and NH2-MIL-53(Al)@BiOBr (AB-x) was prepared without CQDs solution by using the same process. More details are presented in the SI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crystal structures, morphologies, and chemical compositions
ce:section.ce:para: 3D flower-like structure by self-assembled irregular nanosheets of BiOBr as shown in Fig. 1 b. NH2-MIL-53(Al) showed a smooth three-dimensional serration with a radius of approximately 2.5 μm in Fig. 1c. In the nanohybrids (Fig. 1d–e), NH2-MIL-53(Al) as a platform for BiOBr to load, and tightly combine with each other. The lattice fringes of 0.19 nm, 0.27 nm and 0.21 nm in spacing of NH2-MIL-53(Al)@BiOBr@CQDs-10 % correspond to the (111) and (104) crystal planes of BiOBr and the (100) crystal plane of CQDs, indicating the successful synthesis (Fig. 1f) [16]. The energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS) analysis (Fig. 1g), it was found that elements such as Bi, O, C, Al and Br were densely and uniformly present in the NH2-MIL-53(Al)@BiOBr@CQDs-10 %, confirming the successful synthesis of the sample.

Fig. 2 a–b illustrates the XRD patterns of the obtained NH2-MIL-53(Al), BiOBr, AB-x and ABCQDs-x. For the pristine NH2-MIL-53(Al) sample, the diffraction peaks located at 8.8°, 11.7°, 15.2°, 17.6° and 26.5° correspond to (101), (200), (011) (202) and (020) planes of orthorhombic NH2-MIL-53(Al) (JCPDS No.29-0106), respectively [17]. The (001), (101), (102) and (112) planes of tetragonal BiOBr (JCPDS No.09-0393) were attributed to the diffraction representative peaks at 2θ = 10.9°, 25.2°, 31.7°, and 39.4°, respectively [18]. All XRD peaks of the AB-x and ABCQDs-x samples of characteristic peaks be found in main of BiOBr (marked as ♦) and the characteristic 2θ positions of NH2-MIL-53(Al), confirming the formation of heterostructure. The content of NH2-MIL-53(Al) is lower, through amplified picture of XRD (011) and (020) planes fined with increasing the NH2-MIL-53(Al), the peaks of (011) and (020) planes of NH2-MIL-53(Al) gradually strengthen in the compositions.

The bonding and functional groups of different composite AB-x and ABCQDs-x were described by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2c–d). In pure NH2-MIL-53(Al), the peak of C–H bond at 1258 cm−1, peaks at 1400 and 1443 cm−1 were due to symmetric stretching vibration of the carboxyl groups. Peak at 1582 and 1658 cm−1can be considered as the carboxyfunctional group coordinated with Al3+, and the C–N bond of NH2-BDC, and the absorption bands around 3383 and 3494 cm−1 were related to NH2 groups stretching vibration [19]. In pure BiOBr, the peak at 525 cm−1 was assigned to Bi–O bond stretching vibration [20]. The FT-IR spectra of NH2-MIL-53(Al) and BiOBr of ABCQDs-10 % in agreement with reported in literatures, indicating that the samples were successfully prepared.

To further investigate chemical states and interface interactions of NH2-MIL-53(Al), BiOBr and ABCQDs-10 % were characterized by XPS (Fig. 2e–g). Due the Br 3d5/2 and Br 3d3/2 by the Br 3d spin splitting with the peaks at 68.26 eV and 69.16 eV which demonstrating the presence of Br− [21]. The Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 of characteristic peaks of ABCQDs-10 % were consistent with BiOBr, indicating the presence of Bi3+. Two peaks at 160.80 eV and 166.25 eV are observed in the ABCQDs-10 %, which are attributed to the peaks of metal Bi [22,23]. Contrasting with pure BiOBr, the peaks of Br 3d and Bi 4f of ABCQDs-10 % XPS spectra shifted positively. On the contrary to that of Br 3d and Bi 4f, the binding energies of N 1s shifted to the lower values [24]. In general, the decreased of binding energy induce the increased of electron density and electron-screening effect. Under the action force of internal electric, the electrons of ABCQDs-10 % migrated easily from BiOBr to NH2-MIL-53(Al).

More detailed information about the structure of the samples was further investigated by BET surface area analysis. As shown in Fig. 2h, the N2 adsorption-desorption curve of NH2-MIL-53(Al), BiOBr and ABCQDs-10 % showed a type Ⅳ pattern with H1 shaped hysteresis loop, which indicating a classical microporous structure [25,26]. Compared with pure BiOBr (4.2 m2 g−1), the specific surface area of the ABCQDs-10 % (15.1 m2 g−1) is increased, which provide abundant reactive sites to enhance photocatalytic activity.

From the UV–vis DRS shown in Fig. 3 a–b, BiOBr has a higher absorption in ultraviolet light than visible light. Compared with BiOBr, NH2-MIL-53(Al) exhibited a broadened light response range. The absorption intensity of ABCQDs-x was significantly improved in visible region, indicating that NH2-MIL-53(Al) and CQDs were added effectively expand the corresponding range of visible light. Calculations based on Kubelka-Munk function and Tauc curves (Fig. 3c) showed that 2.69 and 2.76 eV were the band gaps (Eg) of NH2-MIL-53(Al) and BiOBr, respectively [27]. The CB potentials vs Ag/AgCl of NH2-MIL-53(Al) and BiOBr were −0.4 and −0.68 eV, respectively, which calculated by the Kubelka-Munk theory (Fig. 3d). The normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) scale obtained by converted the potential vs. Ag/AgCl electrode using Eq. (1).
(1) E(NHE,pH = 7) = E(Ag/AgCl) +0.198− 0.059 × (7- pH of the electrolute)

Therefore, the CB potentials of BiOBr and NH2-MIL-53(Al) were obtained as −0.48 and −0.20 eV vs NHE Eq. (2).
(2) VB = Eg + CB

The valence bands (VB) of BiOBr and NH2-MIL-53(Al) were calculated as 2.28 and 2.49 eV vs NHE, respectively [28,29]. The charge transfer of photocatalysts tested by Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra in Fig. 3e. NH2-MIL-53(Al), BiOBr, AB-10 %, BCQDs and ABCQDs-10 %, ABCDs-10 % exhibited the weakest PL intensity, indicating the lowest the recombination rate of charge and the fastest charge transfer [30,31]. The photocurrent intensity of ABCQDs-10% was higher than that of other samples as shown in Fig. 3f, [32]. This indicated that the faster charge separation and transition in ABCQDs-10 %, which consistent with the results of PL spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The photocatalytic performance and photocatalytic mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the effect of CQDs in catalysis, CIP as contaminants to estimate the photocatalytic performance of AB-x and ABCQDs-x under full-spectrum light (Fig. 4 a–d). The AB-x showed a weak activity, even lower than pure BiOBr, 78.62 %–80.40 % of CIP was degraded within 60 min. This is due to the possible construction of type-II heterojunction of NH2-MIL-53(Al) and BiOBr, leading to electron-hole transfer to energy bands with low redox activity. The degradation of CIP was 86.54 % after BiOBr loaded with CQDs, suggesting that CQDs are beneficial for the transfer of electrons. ABCQDs-x had the better performance than NH2-MIL-53(Al), BiOBr and AB-x. Among them, ABCQDs-10 % showed the excellent performance, and the photocatalytic degradation efficiency achieved 94.24 % within 60 min, which attributed to a Z-type heterojunction with CQDs as the electronic channel and high redox activity. The results were consistent with the PL spectrum. However, due to excessive loading, causing heterojunctions was difficult to constructing, the surface of ABCQDs-10 % was covered and the catalytic activity decreased [33]. The results showed that the rate constant (k) of ABCQDs-10 % was higher than other samples (Fig. 4e–f). The maximum k of ABCQDs-10 % was 0.045 min−1, which was over 64 and 1.2 times more than that of pure NH2-MIL-53(Al) (0.0007 min−1) and BiOBr (0.0376 min−1). Moreover, we conducted a detailed comparison of the research in this paper with previously published papers to clearly highlight the novelty of this study, and the results of the comparison are shown in Table S1. The stability of ABCQDs-10 % was studied and the result was showed in Fig. 4g. The photocatalytic activity of ABCQDs-10 % had not obvious loss after 5 cycles, the degradation decreased slightly attributed to the loss of powder samples during cycling. Additionally, the fresh and used ABCQDs-10 % tested by XRD revealed that the crystal structure and microstructure had no significant changes (Fig. S1).

The active species generated from ABCQDs-10 % were researched through radical trapping experiments on CIP (Fig. 4h). MeOH, BQ, t-BuOH were used as a h+, •O2 - and •OH trapping agent, respectively [34]. The ranked order of contribution of the three radicals to degradation of CIP was⋅•O2 -> h+ > •OH under full-spectrum light illumination in Fig. 4i. Therefore, •O2 -is the main active species during the photocatalytic reaction and was further confirmed by ESR. As demonstrated in Fig. 4i, no DMPO-•O2 - characteristic signal is observed in the dark, while four •O2 - signals increase with prolonged irradiation time. It is due to that the photo generated electrons are transferred to the adsorbed oxygen molecules on the ABCQDs-10 % nanocomposite surface, leading to the formation of •O2 -, which is beneficial to inhibit the interfacial recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes [35,36]. According to the above conclusion, a possible mechanism for the degradation pollutant in Fig. 4g–k.

NH2-MIL-53(Al) and BiOBr composites easy to form type II heterojunction. Since the ECB of NH2-MIL-53(Al) (-0.20 eV) and EVB of BiOBr (2.28 eV) were more positive than Eθ(O2/•O2 -) = -0.33 eV and Eθ(•OH/OH-) = 1.99 eV, respectively, NH2-MIL-53(Al) could not generate •O2 - but BiOBr could generate •OH. CQDs as the electron transfer mediator between NH2-MIL-53(Al) and BiOBr. Subsequently, the low-redox electrons and holes on the CB of NH2-MIL-53(Al) and VB of BiOBr, respectively, combined through the CQDs, the highly redox holes and electrons were reserved, producing large amounts of •O2 - and •OH [37]. The excellent charge separation and transfer performance of CQDs and the construction of highly redox Z-scheme heterojunction resulted in raising charge separation efficiency of ABCQDs-10%.
(3) BiOBr + hν → BiOBr (e− + h+)
(4) NH2-MIL-53(Al) + hν → NH2-MIL-53(Al) (e− + h+)
(5) NH2-MIL-53(Al) (e-)+CQDs → NH2-MIL-53(Al) +CQDs(e-)
(6) CQDs(e-)+ BiOBr → CQDs+ BiOBr (e-)
(7) BiOBr (e-) + O2 → •O2 -
(8) NH2-MIL-53(Al) (h+)+OH- → NH2-MIL-53(Al) + •OH
(9) CIP+h+ /•OH /⋅•O2 - → degradation products(CO2 +H2O)

In summary, ABCQDs (NH2-MIL-53(Al)@BiOBr@CQDs) was synthesized via facile hydrothermal and chemical synthesis processes. The photocatalytic performance of the degradation of CIP as a model molecule. Among them, ABCQDs-10 % had the best removal due to the suitable construction of Z-type heterojunctions which electron-hole separation and transfer efficiency of pollutants. The composite had stability indicated by repeatability experiments. The main active species was⋅•O2 -. Subsequently, photocatalytic mechanism of the composite on CIP was analyzed. This work provides a new approach in degradation of antibiotics.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

Xinyue Yang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yong Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Deyun Yue: Visualization, Investigation, Data curation. Rui Li: Visualization, Software, Investigation, Data curation. Haojie Song: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge funding from National Natural Science Foundation of China (52303376); Key Research and Development Plan of Shaanxi Province (2023-YBGY-429)
